1: YHWH's response to Moses's lament: 'Now you will see what I will do to Pharaoh: because of my mighty hand he will let them go.' The mighty hand (yad chazaqah) is the signature Exodus formula for divine power. Jesus's resurrection is the ultimate mighty act by which the enemy of souls is compelled to release the captives: 'the reason the Son of God appeared was to destroy the works of the devil' (1 John 3:8). The promise 'now you will see' is the promise of vindication after the lament — Easter following Gethsemane.
2: 'I am the LORD' (YHWH) — the bare divine name-claim, unadorned. The progression of revelation is the theme of this chapter: each level of divine self-disclosure is more complete than the last. Jesus's 'I am' declarations (John 6:35; Exodus 8:12; Exodus 10:11; Exodus 11:25; Exodus 14:6; Exodus 15:1) claim the same YHWH-name, applying it to the Son. The final self-disclosure of God takes the form of a human face (2 Corinthians 4:6: 'the light of the knowledge of the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ').
3: 'I appeared to Abraham, to Isaac and to Jacob as God Almighty (El Shaddai), but by my name YHWH I did not make myself fully known to them.' The progressive revelation of the divine name: El Shaddai (the God of the patriarchal promises) is now surpassed by YHWH (the God of covenant rescue). Hebrews 1:1-2 structures this same progression: 'In many and various ways God spoke to our ancestors through the prophets, but in these last days he has spoken to us through his Son.' The Son is the final name — not a word about God but God's own Son who is the Word.
4: The covenant with the patriarchs to give them the land of Canaan is remembered. The covenant memory (zachar) activates the rescue. Paul in 2 Corinthians 1:20 declares: 'All the promises of God find their Yes in him' — every OT covenant promise (Abrahamic, Mosaic, Davidic, New) finds its positive fulfillment in Jesus Christ. The remembered covenant of Exodus 6:4 points forward to the covenant whose mediator and fulfillment is Christ (Hebrews 8:6).
5: YHWH hears the groaning of the enslaved Israelites and remembers his covenant. The God who hears the groan and remembers the covenant is the God whose ear is not deaf to suffering (Psalms 34:18). Jesus in Gethsemane and at the cross enacts this divine compassion from the inside: he takes on the groaning of the enslaved condition (Romans 8:22-23) and from within it, as the one who hears and the one who groans, accomplishes the liberation.
6: The seven 'I will' promises: 'I will bring you out... free you from bondage... redeem you (ga'al) with an outstretched arm (yad netuyah)... I will take you as my people... I will be your God... I will bring you to the land... I will give it to you.' The keyword ga'al (redeemed) is the kinsman-redeemer term: only a member of the family can redeem. Hebrews 2:14-17 applies this logic to the incarnation: 'Since the children share flesh and blood, he too shared in their humanity... For this reason he had to be made like his brothers in every way, in order that he might become a merciful and faithful high priest... making atonement for the sins of the people.' Jesus is the kinsman-redeemer — he became flesh to acquire the right to redeem.
7: 'I will take you as my own people and I will be your God.' This covenant formula — 'you will be my people and I will be your God' — is the thread that runs through the entire biblical narrative, repeated and deepened at each covenant stage (Jeremiah 31:33; Ezekiel 36:28; 2 Corinthians 6:16). Revelation 21:3 is its eschatological fulfillment: 'God's dwelling place is now among the people, and he will dwell with them. They will be his people, and God himself will be with them and be their God.' Christ's work is the means by which this formula becomes permanently true.
8: 'I will bring you to the land I swore with uplifted hand to give to Abraham, to Isaac and to Jacob. I will give it to you as a possession.' The inheritance promise. Jesus as the heir of all things (Hebrews 1:2) inherits the new creation and gives it as an inheritance to his people (Matthew 5:5; Romans 8:17; 1 Peter 1:4). The land of Canaan is the type; the new creation is the antitype. The gift of the inheritance is not earned but sworn — an unconditional divine promise fulfilled through the appointed heir.
9: Moses reports the promises but Israel does not listen 'because of their discouragement and harsh labor' — literally, 'shortness of spirit' (qotzer ruach) and 'hard service.' The crushed spirit that cannot receive good news is the spiritual condition of those under sin's bondage. Jesus came to 'bind up the brokenhearted' (Isaiah 61:1 — the text he reads in Luke 4:18), to release the captives whose spirits have been broken. The problem of Exodus 6:9 is not unbelief in principle but incapacity born of oppression — a pastoral problem that the gospel addresses by the Spirit's liberation.
10: YHWH commissions Moses again despite the failure of the first attempt to reach Israel. The re-commission after failure is the pattern of the entire biblical narrative: Moses after his killing of the Egyptian, Elijah after his flight to Horeb, Peter after his denial. The risen Christ's first post-resurrection word to the disciples is re-commission: 'Go and make disciples' (Matthew 28:19). The commission is not revoked by failure; it is renewed by the risen Lord.
11: 'Go, tell Pharaoh king of Egypt to let the Israelites go out of his country.' The commission repeated with clarity and urgency. The divine word is not withdrawn when it meets resistance; it is reissued. Jesus at Gethsemane chooses the cup — and the commission proceeds to the cross. The word goes forth and does not return empty (Isaiah 55:11).
12: Moses's objection: 'If the Israelites will not listen to me, why would Pharaoh listen to me, since I speak with faltering lips (aral s'fatayim — uncircumcised lips)?' The idiom 'uncircumcised lips' applies the covenant sign of circumcision to the speech organ: Moses's words are, as it were, unredeemed, not set apart. The contrast with Christ could not be sharper: Jesus is the Word of God himself (John 1:1), whose words are spirit and life (John 6:63). The weakness of the flesh (uncircumcised lips) is overcome in Christ who speaks as the Father directed him (John 12:49).
13: YHWH speaks to Moses and Aaron about the Israelites and Pharaoh and commands them to bring the people out of Egypt. The twofold commission — to Israel and to Pharaoh — mirrors the NT apostolic commission that is both to the covenant community (building up the church) and to the powers of the world (proclaiming Christ as Lord over all). The Great Commission (Matthew 28:18-20) operates in both directions: making disciples (Israel) and teaching all nations (Pharaoh-nations).
14: The genealogy begins with Reuben. Levitical genealogies in Exodus serve to establish the priestly lineage, authenticating Aaron's office. The genealogy of Jesus in Matthew 1 and Luke 3 similarly authenticates his identity as the legal heir of David and the biological descendent of Abraham — the legal basis for his messianic office. Both genealogies are not merely historical records but identity-establishing documents that underwrite the authority of the figure they trace.
15: The sons of Simeon listed. The enumeration of Simeon here alongside Reuben situates Levi within the broader tribal structure before the priestly separation is established. Paul in Galatians 3:16-17 argues that the Abrahamic promise was given 430 years before the Sinai law and is not annulled by it — the promises to the patriarchs precede and supersede the Mosaic arrangement. The genealogy traces the human channels of promise, which find their final heir in Christ.
16: 'These were the names of the sons of Levi: Gershon, Kohath, and Merari. Levi lived 137 years.' The Levitical line is established. Hebrews 7:9-10 uses the Levitical genealogy for a remarkable theological argument: Levi paid tithes to Melchizedek through Abraham (since Levi was 'still in the loins' of Abraham). If the Levitical priesthood — established in this very genealogy — is itself subordinate to Melchizedek's order, then Christ's priesthood in the order of Melchizedek (Psalms 110:4) surpasses the Levitical order that Exodus 6:16 here traces.
17: The sons of Gershon: Libni and Shimei. These clan names fill out the Levitical clan structure that organizes Israel's worship throughout the wilderness period. In the new covenant, there is no Levitical clan system mediating access to God; 'there is one God and one mediator between God and humanity, the man Christ Jesus' (1 Timothy 2:5). The complexity of the Levitical structure is both honored and transcended in the single perfect mediator.
18: The sons of Kohath: Amram, Izhar, Hebron, and Uzziel. The Kohathites are the family that carries the most holy objects of the tabernacle (Numbers 4:4-20). They carry the ark, the table, the lampstand, and the altars — covered and wrapped, never touching them directly, or they die (Numbers 4:15). The holy objects must not be handled directly by fallen human hands. Christ as the true ark (the mercy seat — Romans 3:25), the true light of the world (John 8:12), and the true altar (Hebrews 13:10) is approached not through the mediation of Kohathite priests but directly through faith.
19: The sons of Merari: Mahli and Mushi. The Merarites are responsible for the tabernacle's structural elements — boards, bars, posts, bases. The physical structure of YHWH's dwelling place among his people requires laborers for the frame. Christ as the cornerstone (Ephesians 2:20; 1 Peter 2:6-7) is the structural element on which the whole edifice of the church rests — not a priest carrying holy objects but the foundation that makes the building possible.
20: Amram married Jochebed his father's sister, and she bore Aaron and Moses. Amram lived 137 years. The parentage of Moses and Aaron is here given official genealogical status. The priestly dynasty originates in a specific historical family. Jesus's genealogy in Luke 3 traces back through all generations to Adam — emphasizing his solidarity with all humanity — while Matthew's begins with Abraham, emphasizing his fulfillment of covenant promise. The genealogies establish the human credentials of the one whose divine identity needs no credential.
21: The sons of Izhar: Korah, Nepheg, and Zichri. Korah will later lead the rebellion against Moses and Aaron (Num 16) — the established priestly line itself becomes the source of sacerdotal rebellion. Jude 11 cites 'Korah's rebellion' as the type of those who abandon the divine order for self-appointed leadership. The failure within the priestly line points to the need for a priest of a different order, one who holds his office permanently 'because he always lives to intercede' (Hebrews 7:25).
22: The sons of Uzziel: Mishael, Elzaphan, and Sithri. Elzaphan son of Uzziel is later commanded to carry the bodies of Nadab and Abihu from the sanctuary after their unauthorized fire (Leviticus 10:4-5). The priestly family is implicated in both faithful service and the consequences of sacrilegious innovation. The failed and fallible human priesthood constantly requires a succession of replacements — pointing forward to the one who 'holds his priesthood permanently, because he continues forever' (Hebrews 7:24).
23: Aaron married Elisheba daughter of Amminadab, sister of Nahshon, who bore Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, and Ithamar. Nahshon son of Amminadab appears in the genealogies of Matthew 1:4 and Luke 3:32-33 as an ancestor of David and thus of Jesus. The family connection between the high-priestly line (Aaron-Elisheba) and the Davidic messianic line (Nahshon-Amminadab) is genealogically embedded here — a hint of the coming one who unites in himself the royal and priestly offices (Ps 110; Zechariah 6:13; Hebrews 7:1-3).
24: The sons of Korah: Assir, Elkanah, and Abiasaph. The Korahites, despite their ancestor's rebellion, become honored temple servants — 'the sons of Korah' compose Psalms 42, 44-49, 84-85, 87-88. Divine grace reverses judgment within families: the descendants of a rebel become worshippers. The cross is the ultimate instance of this reversal — the very instrument of death becomes the seat of redemption; the one executed as a criminal is Lord of all.
25: Eleazar son of Aaron married a daughter of Putiel and she bore Phinehas. These were the heads of the Levitical families. Phinehas will be credited with righteousness for his zealous action at Baal Peor (Numbers 25:7-13; Psalms 106:30-31), demonstrating that zeal for YHWH's honor is itself a form of covenant faithfulness. Paul in Romans 10:2-4 distinguishes zeal without knowledge (which misses Christ) from the 'righteousness that comes from God' received through faith — the trajectory from Phinehas's zeal to Christ's perfect righteousness.
26: 'It was this same Aaron and Moses to whom the LORD said: bring the Israelites out of Egypt by their divisions.' The validation of the commission — these are the authorized agents. Jesus at the beginning of his ministry receives divine validation: 'This is my Son, whom I love; with him I am well pleased' (Matthew 3:17). The Father's voice authenticates the mission before it begins; the genealogy here authenticates Moses and Aaron before the plagues and the exodus. Authorization precedes execution.
27: 'They were the ones who spoke to Pharaoh about bringing the Israelites out — this same Moses and Aaron.' The identification is emphatic: these specific individuals, not generic types. The incarnation is equally specific: 'the Word became flesh and dwelt among us' (John 1:14) — a particular person, at a particular time, in a particular place. Salvation comes through specific people commissioned by God, not through general religious ideas. The specificity of Moses-and-Aaron typifies the specificity of Jesus of Nazareth as the one mediator.
28: 'On the day the LORD spoke to Moses in Egypt.' The temporal anchor: the divine word spoken on a specific day. John's Gospel begins with 'In the beginning was the Word' — the eternal Word who enters time. The day YHWH spoke to Moses is a type of the day the Word became flesh (John 1:14) — the eternal divine speech taking temporal, historical form through a specific human agent.
29: 'I am the LORD. Tell Pharaoh king of Egypt everything I tell you.' The commission formula restated: the identity declaration (I am YHWH) followed by the imperative of total transmission. Jesus says in John 12:49-50: 'I did not speak on my own, but the Father who sent me commanded me to say all that I have spoken.' The perfect prophet-mediator transmits everything — nothing withheld, nothing added. Moses's total commission from YHWH is fulfilled in Jesus's total transmission of the Father's words.
30: 'Since I speak with faltering lips, why would Pharaoh listen to me?' Moses's objection returns — the chapter ends where it began (Exodus 6:12), framing the genealogy with the problem of inadequate human agency. The answer to this question is the entire gospel narrative: because the Word became flesh (John 1:14), because God sent his own Son (Romans 8:3), the message goes forth not in the weakness of a reluctant human speaker but in the power of the eternal Word who addresses Pharaoh-the-world through the weakness of the cross (1 Corinthians 1:25).
